1: Isaiah 13 begins the 'Burden' oracles (massa, burden/oracle — the term for a prophetic message heavy with judgment) that run through Isaiah 13-23: Babylon (13-14), Philistia (Isaiah 14:28-32), Moab (15-16), Damascus (17), Egypt (19), Babylon again (21), Edom (Isaiah 21:11-12), Arabia (Isaiah 21:13-17), Jerusalem (22), Tyre (23). The first and longest concerns Babylon — unusual for an 8th-century prophet since Babylon was not yet the dominant power in Isaiah's time (that came in the 7th-6th century with Nebuchadnezzar). Isaiah's Babylon oracle anticipates the exile that Isaiah 40-55 address — a prophetic reach forward that the book's canonical structure affirms.
2: 'Raise a banner on the bare mountain! Lift your voice to them! Wave your hand, so that they may march through the gates of the rulers!' — the mustering signal: the banner (nes) raised on the bare mountain (har-nishpeh, a bald/cleared mountain top — a signal fire or flag visible from afar) is YHWH's military deployment command. This is the same signal as Isaiah 5:26 (YHWH whistling for the distant nation) and Isaiah 11:10-12 (the banner for the ingathering). Here the signal deploys the divine army against Babylon. 'The gates of the rulers' (she'arei nedivim, the gates of nobles) = the city gates of Babylon's aristocracy — the invading force will enter through Babylon's own gates.
3: 'I have given orders to my consecrated troops; I have summoned my warriors to carry out my wrath — those who exult in my majesty' — YHWH's consecrated/holy troops (meqodeshai, my holy ones = warriors set apart for holy war; cf. Numbers 31:6; Jeremiah 6:4 where the command to 'consecrate war' = haqdishu milchamah). The divine warrior is calling up his host: both the heavenly armies and the historical Medes who will conquer Babylon (v17). 'Those who exult in my majesty' (alizehai ga'avati, exulting in my pride) — YHWH's warriors who take pride in divine glory, not their own. The consecrated-warrior language contrasts with the Assyrian troops who boasted in their own power (Isaiah 10:13-14).
4: 'Listen — the roar of multitudes on the mountains, like a vast army! The clamour of kingdoms and nations massing together! The LORD of hosts is assembling his battle force' — the auditory vision: qol hamon baharim, demut am-rav (the sound of a multitude on the mountains, the likeness of a great people). The 'multitude on the mountains' is the sound before sight — the army heard at a distance. 'The LORD of hosts is assembling his battle force' (Yhwh tseva'ot mefahed tzeva milchamah) — YHWH mustering his army (milchamah, war/battle forces) is the eschatological war assembly. The combination of 'kingdoms and nations massing together' suggests a force beyond any single national army — the divine coalition exceeds human military categories.
5: 'They are coming from a distant land, from the far end of the heavens — the LORD himself with the instruments of his wrath — to lay waste to the whole earth' — 'from the far end of the heavens' (miqqetseh hashamayim, from the extremity of the heavens) — the divine army's origin point is beyond human geography, from the edge of the cosmos. 'The LORD himself with the instruments of his wrath' (Yhwh ukelei za'amo, YHWH himself and the instruments of his indignation) identifies the army's composition: YHWH commanding human agents who are his weapons. 'The whole earth' (kol-ha'arets) as the target (not just Babylon) signals that the Babylon oracle will expand to universal eschatological judgment, as Isaiah 13:9-13 confirm.
6: 'Wail, for the day of the LORD is close at hand — it will come as devastation from the Almighty' — the Day of the LORD (yom Yhwh) announced for Babylon: the same theme as Amos 5:18-20 (woe to those who desire the day of the LORD) and Joel 1-2. 'Devastation from the Almighty' (shod mishaddai, destruction from Shaddai) is a wordplay: shod (devastation) sounds like Shaddai (the Almighty's ancient title). The same wordplay appears in Joel 1:15: 'The day of the LORD is coming. It will come as a destruction from the Almighty.' El-Shaddai (the powerful God of the patriarchs, Genesis 17:1; Isaiah 35:11) becomes the source of shod — the God of ancient covenant blessing becomes the destroyer of his enemies.
7: 'At this every hand will go limp and every human heart will melt with fear' — the universal physical response to the Day of the LORD: 'every hand will go limp' (kol-yadayim tirpeh, all hands become flaccid) and 'every heart will melt' (kol-levav enosh yimmas, every heart of man melts). The melting heart is the terror-response in battle (Deuteronomy 20:8; Joshua 2:11: Rahab reports that 'our hearts melted in fear' when Israel approached; Joshua 5:1: 'all the Amorite and Canaanite kings melted in terror'). These are the same terror-responses that YHWH promised to send against Israel's enemies in holy war — now applied to those experiencing YHWH's own judgment.
8: 'They will be seized with anguish; pangs and agony will grip them; they will writhe like a woman in labour. They will look at one another in horror, their faces burning red' — three word-groups for physical agony: tziruth vecharalot (pangs and convulsions), yacholun kaioledat (they writhe like a woman in labor), ish el-re'ehu yitmehu (each looks at his neighbor in amazement/horror). The woman-in-labor comparison for sudden, unavoidable agony is a recurring prophetic image for military catastrophe (Jeremiah 6:24; Isaiah 13:21; Isaiah 22:23; Isaiah 50:43; Micah 4:9-10). Paul uses the same labor imagery in 1 Thessalonians 5:3: 'destruction will come suddenly, as labor pains on a pregnant woman,' but reverses the application to the unexpected arrival of the Day of the Lord for the unbeliever.
9: 'See, the day of the LORD is coming — relentless in wrath and burning anger — to turn the land into a ruin and wipe out every sinner from it' — the Day of the LORD defined by two attributes: achzar (cruel/relentless) and chemah ve'evron (fury and burning anger). The double-anger vocabulary (chemah = hot anger; evron = overflowing rage/wrath) intensifies the heat-metaphor already in the burning/scorching language. The goal: 'to turn the land into a ruin' (lasim ha'arets leshamamah, to make the land desolate) and 'wipe out every sinner' (vachata'eha yishmid mimmenah, sinners he will destroy from it). The desolation-and-sinner-removal dual purpose marks this as both ecological and moral judgment.
10: 'The stars of heaven and their constellations will not give their light; the sun will be dark when it rises and the moon will not shed its glow' — the cosmic darkening: the celestial lights extinguished. 'Stars' (kochavei hashamayim), 'constellations' (ucsileihem, their Kesils/Orions — kesil is both Orion the constellation and 'fool/obstinate one'), the sun (shemesh) dark at rising, and the moon (yareach) not giving its light. These cosmic signs are the reversal of Genesis 1:14-16 (luminaries created to give light). The same imagery recurs in Joel 2:10; Isaiah 3:15; Matthew 24:29; Luke 21:25; Revelation 6:12-13 as the signs of the great eschatological Day. The darkening of the luminaries signals the end of the cosmic order sustained since creation.
11: 'I will punish the world for its evil and the wicked for their guilt; I will put an end to the arrogance of the proud and cut down the insolence of tyrants' — the expansion from Babylon-specific to universal judgment: 'the world' (tevel, the habitable earth) is now the scope of YHWH's punishment. The two specific targets: ga'on (arrogance/pride of the proud) and gevah aritsim (haughtiness of tyrants/violent ones) — the same pride-vocabulary as Isaiah 2:10-22 (the Day-of-the-LORD against everything proud). The Babylon oracle thus becomes a particular instance of the universal Day-of-the-LORD theme that Isaiah 2 announced: Babylon is the concentrated expression of human pride against which YHWH acts.
12: 'Human life will become rarer than pure gold — rarer even than the gold of Ophir' — the scarcity of survivors: okar enosh miyaqar (I will make a man more precious than refined gold) and adam mizahav Ofir (a man more than the gold of Ophir). Gold of Ophir was the finest gold in the ancient Near East (1 Kings 9:28: Solomon brought 420 talents of Ophir gold; Psalms 45:9: 'fine gold from Ophir'). The rarity metaphor inverts the Day of the LORD's normal population imagery: instead of swarming armies (Isaiah 5:26-30; Isaiah 13:4), survivors will be as rare as precious metal. The few survivors after judgment are the remnant theology applied to Babylon's judgment.
13: 'I will make the heavens tremble, and the earth will be displaced from its foundations by the wrath of the LORD of hosts on the day of his burning anger' — the cosmic shaking: 'the heavens tremble' (arzil hashamayim, I will shake the heavens) and 'the earth will be displaced from its foundations' (vetirash ha'arets mimqomah, the earth will be shaken from its place). The cosmic foundations of both heaven and earth destabilized by divine anger (beyom charone aph Yhwh, on the day of YHWH's burning nostril-anger). Revelation 6:12-17 draws on this for the sixth seal's cosmic earthquake: 'the sky receded like a scroll being rolled up, and every mountain and island was moved from its place.' The theophanic shaking of Psalms 18:7 and Habakkuk 3:6 is the background.
14: 'Like a hunted gazelle or like sheep scattered with no one to round them up, each person will flee back to his own people, each run to his own homeland' — the flight: the people of Babylon's empire scatter like a gazelle before a hunter (tsvi, the graceful deer notorious for its speed in flight) or sheep with no shepherd (tso'n ve'ein meqabets, sheep with no one gathering them). Each person flees to their own native population and land — the diaspora communities of the empire dissolving back to ethnic origins. This reflects the actual Cyrus Cylinder policy: Cyrus allowed deported peoples to return to their homelands, including the Jews (Ezra 1:1-4).
15: 'Anyone who is caught will be run through with a sword; anyone taken prisoner will fall by the blade' — the gruesome fate of those who cannot flee: run through (yiddaqer, pierced/stabbed) and fallen by the sword (yippol becherev). The passive verbs acknowledge the overwhelming military force against which there is no resistance. This is the standard ancient Near Eastern description of city conquest: the Assyrian reliefs and annals both document the same treatment of captured soldiers and civilians. For Babylon's fall to Cyrus (539 BCE), the historical record suggests a relatively bloodless conquest, but the oracle's scope is eschatological: the final judgment on all who oppose YHWH.
16: 'Their children will be dashed to pieces in front of them; their homes will be looted and their wives raped' — the most brutal element of ancient siege warfare applied to Babylon: children dashed to pieces (olalehem yirutteshu, their infants will be dashed), houses looted (batteihem yeshosseshu, their homes will be plundered), wives ravished (venesheihem tishakavnah, their wives will lie with men = be raped). These are the standard covenant curses of Deuteronomy 28:30 (your wife taken by another man) applied at their most extreme. Psalms 137:9 ('blessed is the one who seizes your infants and dashes them against the rocks') voices the same retributive justice against Babylon from the Judean exile's perspective.
17: 'I am rousing the Medes against Babylon — a people who care nothing for silver and feel no pull toward gold' — the specific human instrument: the Medes (Madai, the Indo-European people of northwestern Iran who allied with Babylon to destroy Assyria in 612 BCE at Nineveh, and later participated in Cyrus's Persian victory over Babylon in 539 BCE). The specific characterization 'who care nothing for silver' (lo yachshofu keseph, they do not account silver) suggests a motivation other than plunder — the Medes/Persians under Cyrus were portrayed as liberators (cf. the Cyrus Cylinder), not looters. The historical fulfillment: Cyrus II conquered Babylon in October 539 BCE with little resistance, famously diverting the Euphrates to breach Babylon's defenses.
18: 'Their bows will cut the young men down; they will show no mercy on newborn infants; they will have no pity for children' — the Medes' military ferocity applied universally: bows (qeshet) cutting down young men (yenatelashu ne'arim, they will dash them), no mercy on the fruit of the womb (lo yerachem al-peri beten, they will not show compassion on the fruit of the womb), no pity for children (lo tachov einam al-banim, their eye will not have pity on boys). The triple expression of mercilessness mirrors the triple expression of Babylon's punishment in v16. The instrument of judgment (Medes) is as harsh as the judgment deserves.
19: 'Babylon — the glory of kingdoms, the pride and beauty of the Chaldeans — will be like Sodom and Gomorrah when God overturned them' — the Sodom comparison applied to Babylon: as YHWH 'overturned' (kemahpechat Elohim, like the overthrow of God = divine overthrow) Sodom and Gomorrah, so Babylon. The threefold description of Babylon's glory: 'glory of kingdoms' (tzvi mamlachot, the splendor/gazelle of kingdoms), 'pride of the Chaldeans' (tiferet ge'on Kasdim, the beauty of the pride of the Chaldeans), and 'beauty' (the repetition for emphasis). Ancient Babylon was one of the ancient world's most impressive cities — Herodotus describes its massive walls, the Ishtar Gate, and its hanging gardens. The comparison to Sodom promises complete annihilation.
20: 'It will never be lived in or settled again through all generations; no Bedouin will pitch a tent there, no shepherds will let their flocks rest there' — the permanent desolation oracle: lo-teshev lanetsach velo tishkon (it will never be inhabited and never be dwelt in) through all generations (ledor vador, from generation to generation). The two specific witnesses to permanence: no Bedouin tent (ohel lo-ya'al sham aravi, the Arab/nomad will not pitch his tent there) and no shepherd resting flocks (ro'im lo yarbitsun sham, shepherds will not let their flocks lie down there). In the ancient world, abandoned ruins typically served as campgrounds for nomads — if not even Bedouins will camp there, the desolation is complete.
21: 'Desert creatures will take up residence there; their empty houses will be full of owls; ostriches will make their home there and wild goats will frolic there' — the ecological catalogue of desolation: tsiyyim (desert creatures/hyenas), ochim (owls), yene'im (ostriches), se'irim (wild goats/demons — se'ir can mean hairy ones, goats, or the goat-demons of Leviticus 17:7 and 2 Chronicles 11:15). The animals that inhabit ruins rather than cultivated land are the index of complete abandonment. This is the reverse of Ps 104's ecological catalogue of fruitful creation — the peaceable kingdom of Isaiah 11 inverted into the chaotic wilderness of Babylon's ruins. Revelation 18:2 applies similar language to the eschatological Babylon: 'Fallen! Fallen is Babylon the Great! She has become a dwelling for demons and a haunt for every impure spirit.'
22: 'Hyenas will cry out in her citadels and jackals in her splendid palaces. Her time is about to come; her days will not be drawn out' — the completion of the desolation catalogue: iyim (howling jackals/hyenas) in her citadels (be'armenoteiha, in her fortified palaces/citadels) and tannim (jackals/dragons) in the 'temples of pleasure' (beheichalei oneg, in the pleasure-palaces). The contrast between the original purpose (citadels of power, pleasure-palaces of the rich) and the actual inhabitants (wild animals) completes the reversal of Babylonian pride. 'Her time is about to come' (ve'arob ittah, her time is near) and 'her days will not be drawn out' (uyameha lo ye'arechun, her days will not be prolonged) — the closing urgency note: Babylon's end is certain and imminent.
